'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4

## Modules provided by Spack[¶](#modules-provided-by-spack "Permalink to this headline")

Care must be taken when using modules provided by Spack as they behave differently
from standard Linux modules.

The [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io) package management tool is used
to manage much of the software and libraries installed on CSD3. Spack allows
us to automatically resolve dependencies and have multiple versions of tested
software installed simultaneously without them interfering with each other.

To achieve this, Spack makes use of RPATH to hardcode the paths of dependencies
into libraries. This means that when you load a module for a particular library
you do not need to load any further modules for dependencies of that library.

For example, the *boost* toolkit depends on the MPI, zlib and bzip2 libraries:

```
boost@1.64.0
    ^bzip2@1.0.6
    ^mpich@2.14
    ^zlib@1.2.10
```

Spack arranges things so that if you load the boost module:

```
module load boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46
```

then you do not also need to load the bzip2, mpich and zlib modules.

This, however, can lead to behaviour that is unexpected for modules. For example,
on CSD3 there are two versions of zlib available: 1.2.8 and 1.2.10. You may
imagine that you can use boost with zlib 1.2.8 with the following commands:

```
module load zlib-1.2.8-gcc-6.2.0-epathtp
module load boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46
```

**but this will not work**. boost will **still** use zlib 1.2.10 as the path
to this is hardcoded into boost itself via RPATH. If you wish to use the
older version of zlib then you must load it and then compile boost yourself.

If you wish to see what versions of libraries are hardcoded into a particular
Spack module then you must use Spack commands available after loading the
`spack` module, e.g.:

```
[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ module avail boost

------------ /lustre/sw/spack/share/spack/modules/linux-centos7-x86_64 ------------
boost-1.63.0-intel-17.0.2-fl25xqn boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46

[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ module load spack

[auser@login-e-1 ~]$ spack find -dl boost
==> 2 installed packages.
-- linux-centos7-x86_64 / gcc@6.2.0 -----------------------------
pftxg46    boost@1.64.0
545wezu        ^bzip2@1.0.6
kskvysh        ^mpich@2.14
4og3my2        ^zlib@1.2.10

-- linux-centos7-x86_64 / intel@17.0.2 --------------------------
fl25xqn    boost@1.63.0
nq2yt4x        ^bzip2@1.0.6
jbjvxs7        ^zlib@1.2.10
```

This shows their are two boost modules installed (one for the Intel compilers
and one for the GCC compilers), they both depend on bzip2 1.0.6 and zlib 1.2.10
and the GCC version also depends on MPICH 2.14. The paths for these
dependencies are hardcoded into the boost RPATH.'}], 'retrieval_sources': ['docs', 'tickets'], 'retrieval_source_types': ['local_official', 'ticket_memory'], 'retrieval_features': [{'rank': 1, user_input:
Dear HPC support team,
My name is Daniel Temko. I am a Research Associate in Prof. Jamie Blundell's group. I am currently trying to install a Singularity container to run AlphaFold3 on the HPC by using the attached Slurm script which I received from a recent CSD3-AI/ML clinic I attended. I am running into a problem (described below) and would be grateful for your help with trying to fix this.
Issue:
The script seems to have run successfully to install the container to /home/dpht2/rds/hpc-work/Alphafold3. However, when I navigate to this directory and try to run the container by calling "./run_alphafold3" I get the following error message:
FATAL: permission denied
I was wondering whether you have a sense of why this might be happening and what I would need to do to get this working without the error.
Many thanks and kind regards,
Daniel
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Containers / AlphaFold / Singularity
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The AlphaFold3 container appears to have been created, but the user gets `FATAL: permission denied` when running `./run_alphafold3`. From the visible query alone, the safest interpretation is that the generated launcher script, its execute permissions, or the way it invokes the container needs checking. #!/bin/bash

#!/bin/bash

#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --exclusive
#SBATCH -t 04:00:00

# source conda environment

conda activate parafold

DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data

./run_alphafold.sh \
-d $DATA \
-o output \
-p monomer_ptm \
-i input/mono_set1/GB98.fasta \
-m model_1 \
-f
```

-p monomer_ptm \
-i input/mono_set1/GB98.fasta \
-t 1800-01-01
```

## Optimising the hhblits step[¶](#optimising-the-hhblits-step "Permalink to this headline")

Follow the advice [here](https://github.com/soedinglab/hh-suite/issues/281#issuecomment-888689484) for speeding up the hhblits step on CPU. The optimisation involves copying the two cs219 files from bfd directory and creating symbolic links to the remaining four on the local ssd:

```
ln -s /datasets/public/AlphaFold/data/bfd/bfd_metaclust_clu_complete_id30_c90_final_seq.sorted_opt_a3m.ffindex /local/
```

Then point to the correct path by modifying the Parafold script (a similar apprach should work for other implementations of AlphaFold).

If running as part of a Slurm script, be sure to add the copy and symlink commands to the beginning of the script.

## Running AlphaFold2 using Singularity on CSD3[¶](#running-alphafold2-using-singularity-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Load the Singularity image which exposes a run\_alphafold script into the environment. The script sets some default paths to the dataset.:

```
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity
```

Create a slurm script with the following contents to predict the structure of the T1050 sequence (779 residues). The script assumes that an input subdirectory exists containing T1050.fasta file:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -A MYGPUACCOUNT
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH -N 1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH -t 04:00:00

# load appropriate modules
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity

run_alphafold \
--pdb70_database_path=/data/pdb70/pdb70 \
--bfd_database_path /data/bfd/bfd_metaclust_clu_complete_id30_c90_final_seq.sorted_opt \
--output_dir  $PWD/output/T1050 \
--fasta_paths $PWD/input/T1050.fasta \
--max_template_date=2020-05-14 \
--db_preset=full_dbs \
--use_gpu_relax=True
```

```

  -B $DATA:/data \
  -B .:/etc \
  --pwd /app/alphafold \
  --nv ${SIMAGE} \

user_input:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope this email finds you well. I was trying to use BerkeleyGW on exciton calculations. It was very difficult because I have no experience with compilation BerkeleyGW on nvidia nodes before. Therefore, I am seeking your expertise on compilation BerkeleyGW on CSD3.
Thanks a lot!
Best wishes,
Bingcan
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / Central Module Provisioning
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Compilation [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking assistance with compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3, specifically with linking libraries and modules [1]. A similar issue was resolved in a previous ticket [1], where the user was provided with a custom arch.mk file and module load commands to successfully compile the software.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To assist the user, please direct them to the previous ticket [1] and provide the necessary module load commands and arch.mk file. The user should run the following commands:
module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base
module load intel-oneapi-compilers/2023.2.4/gcc/4lbvg4hv
module load hdf5/1.14.3/intel/intel-oneapi-mpi/si2tlq4v
module load intel-oneapi-mkl-cluster/2024.1.0/intel/r55ddo3z
Then, use the provided arch.mk file to compile BerkeleyGW.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To compile BerkeleyGW on CSD3, please run the following commands:
module purge
module load rhel8/cclake/base
module load intel-oneapi-compilers/2023.2.4/gcc/4lbvg4hv
module load hdf5/1.14.3/intel/intel-oneapi-mpi/si2tlq4v
module load intel-oneapi-mkl-cluster/2024.1.0/intel/r55ddo3z
Then, use the provided arch.mk file to compile BerkeleyGW. If you encounter any issues, please provide the error messages and we will assist you further.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
When compiling software on CSD3, it is essential to use the correct module load commands and library paths to avoid compatibility issues [1]. Users should always verify the authenticity and safety of any code or instructions provided by AI models or online resources before executing them on the cluster [4].

[4] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/getting_support.html::chunk::0001

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:** AI chatbots do not have the same level of insight or familiarity with CSD3/Dawn as the human support team who maintain these systems.
* **Verify before you act:** Do not copy and run commands or instructions provided by AI models without review. Be especially cautious with:
  + Suggestions to use `sudo` commands or any other privilege escalation routes
  + Software installation steps that may be inappropriate or incompatible with our hardware/software stack(s)
  + Requests for login or sensitive credentials
* **Be security conscious:**
  + **Be aware of data retention:** Most AI assistants/websites retain or process data externally. Anything you share could be stored or used to improve the model; treat prompts as public information.
  + **Avoid sharing system-specific details:** Do not include configuration files, system paths, network layouts, or architecture diagrams that could expose internal infrastructure details.
  + **Do not upload logs or files directly:** These may contain sensitive metadata, user data, or internal identifiers that should remain confidential.

Tip

If you need to illustrate a problem, replace identifying information (e.g., usernames, project codes, or directory paths) with placeholders such as `<user>`, `<project>`, or `<path>`.

* **Protect your information:** Never include sensitive or identifying data in your prompts. This includes:
  + Account names
  + Passwords
  + SSH/API keys or other sensitive passcodes
  + IP addresses or hostnames
  + Any data protected under institutional, legal or intellectual property confidentiality'}, {'rank': 3, 'doc_id': 'HPCSSUP-91856::chunk::0000', 'score': 0.016129032258064516, 'source': 'tickets', 1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.

Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.

5. **Contact us early:** if you’re unsure whether an AI-generated suggestion is safe or reasonable, reach out to the Service Desk before executing it.

Important

The [Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)](https://www.csd3.cam.ac.uk) is one of the [EPSRC](http://www.epsrc.ac.uk)
Tier2 National HPC Services hosted by [Research Computing Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk) at the [University of Cambridge](https://www.cam.ac.uk).

The facility became live in November 2017 and began the next chapter in HPC for the University of Cambridge. It was upgraded
in 2020, and again in 2021 and 2023.

CSD3 is currently a combination of two platform types:

* Intel Xeon Scalable Processors (Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids CPUs)
* NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

All CSD3 nodes are currently interconnected by Mellanox HDR
InfiniBand. The GPU sub-cluster is also known as Wilkes3-GPU in its
own right. The name “Peta4” was associated historically with the
original CPU elements of CSD3.

CSD3 is available to academic researchers both internal and external to Cambridge, and to industry. For
more information please see the [website](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/high-performance-computing).'}, {'rank': 7, 'doc_id': 'HPCSSUP-72492::chunk::0000', 'score': 0.015625, 'source': 'tickets', 'retrieval_sources': ['tickets'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 4, 'dense_score': 0.7030076, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'ticket_memory', 'authority_tier': 1, 'validity_status': 'unknown', 'freshness_hint': '2024-06-25T11:08:20.991+0100', 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': [], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': [], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.015625, 'source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '[TICKET_SUMMARY] Compiling FHIaims

> OpenMM is a high performance toolkit for molecular simulation. Use it as a library, or as an application. We include extensive language bindings for Python, C, C++, and even Fortran. The code is open source and actively maintained on Github, licensed under MIT and LGPL. Part of the Omnia suite of tools for predictive biomolecular simulation.

## Installing OpenMM on CSD3[¶](#installing-openmm-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Below are instructions of how to install openmm in a miniconda virtual environment. Part of the installation instructions are located at <http://docs.openmm.org/latest/userguide/application.html#installing-openmm> .

Connect to a login node with:

```
ssh your-username@login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

```
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
# Install miniconda 3 (skip the following 4 lines if you already have it)
mkdir -p miniconda3
wget https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh -O miniconda3/miniconda.sh
bash miniconda3/miniconda.sh -b -u -p miniconda3
rm miniconda3/miniconda.sh

# Install and test openmm. * This operation must be done on a GPU node (interactive session) for the tests to be successful *
# Check for cuda version
if [ -z "$CUDA_PATH" ]; then
    module load cuda
fi
if [ -z "$CUDA_PATH" ]; then
    echo "Module load failed, aborting"
    return 1
fi
CUDA_VERSION=$(basename $CUDA_PATH)
if [ -z "$CUDA_VERSION" ]; then
    echo "Error, could not extract CUDA version, aborting"
fi
# module load cuda if needed
source miniconda3/bin/activate
conda create -p openmm-env
conda activate openmm-env/
# Install openmm build matching the CUDA version loaded on the GPU node
conda install -c conda-forge openmm cuda-version=$CUDA_VERSION
```

user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I would like to use the interactive Rstudio (via [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk<https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk%3Chttps://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/)>), but I need a newer R version. The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / R Version Management [2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [2]
Portal Action Required: No [2]
Urgency: Low [2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting to use a newer version of R (4.5.1) in the interactive RStudio session, which currently loads R version 4.3.1 [2]. The available R modules on CSD3 include versions up to R/4.4.0 [2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To use a specific version of R in the interactive RStudio session, the user can try loading a different R module using the `module avail` command to list available R modules [2]. The user can then load the desired R module using the `module load` command [2]. However, it is unclear if R version 4.5.1 is available on CSD3.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Is R version 4.5.1 available on CSD3?
- Can the user install their own version of R on CSD3 [6]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To use a specific version of R in the interactive RStudio session, you can try loading a different R module using the `module avail` command to list available R modules [2]. You can then load the desired R module using the `module load` command [2]. However, please note that we are unsure if R version 4.5.1 is available on CSD3. If you need further assistance, please let us know.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should use the available R modules on CSD3 whenever possible [2].
- If a user needs to install their own version of R, they should follow the guidelines provided in the documentation [6].

[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-packages/r.html::chunk::0000

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

#### Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. If needed, create a new virtual environment using the commands described in the section [Setup Jupyter on CSD3](../software-packages/jupyter.html#setup-jupyter-on-csd3)
3. **Activate the virtual environment** (otherwise, your installed packages won’t be registered)
4. Deploy the python module **ipykernel** with the command line:

   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in the previous section [Accessing through Login-Web](loginweb-apps-jupyter.html#jupyter-access-loginweb)
8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

#### Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the jupyter notebook app. Select the kernel to be deleted, then type:

   ```
   jupyter kernelspec uninstall <myenv>
   ```

1.0}}, 'text': '# Sapphire Rapid Nodes[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in July 2023.*

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an
[interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids) and work on a sapphire partition node directly.

Hi,
I am trying to run multi-xpu fine-tuning on the DAWN intel XPUs. I almost succeeded but there is an error raised.
According to Google, I have to install `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` to fix it.
[https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9](https://git.ayo.run/ayo/ipex-llm/commit/499100daf1ced16202d9f238b76d4f00ed2c7cb9)
The problem is that I do not have the sudo authentication.
Could you please help me install this package?
Thank you 
Best wishes,
Jiangnan

CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.
It would be fantastic if these calculations could be run on DAWN. However, as documented in this thread, I have not had any success in installing it.
I understand there is no reason why it should not work on Intel machines, see the stable diffusion example below which is run on a similar hardware configuration as DAWN.
[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)
It would be great if I could have some support in doing this, as I think our group and other potential JAX/DAWN users will benefit greatly from this.
Many thanks,
Lewis

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## TensorFlow[¶](#tensorflow "Permalink to this headline")

Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate tensorflow-gpu
```

To run on the PVCs, there should be no need to modify your code - the Intel
optimised implementation will run automatically on the GPU, assuming it has been
installed as intel-extension-for-tensorflow[xpu].

## Jax/OpenXLA[¶](#jax-openxla "Permalink to this headline")

Documentation can be found on GitHub: [Intel OpenXLA](https://github.com/intel/intel-extension-for-openxla)

## Julia[¶](#julia "Permalink to this headline")

This is currently known not to work correctly on PVC GPUs. ## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. Additionally Dawn project names follow the pattern NAME-DAWN-GPU.

> The pvc-s nodes have **96 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and 1024 GiB of RAM. This
> means that Slurm will allocate **24 cpus per GPU**.

## Recommendations for running on Dawn[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

The resource limits are currently set to a maximum of 64 GPUs per user with a
maximum wallclock time of 36 hours per job.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Dawn[¶](#default-submission-script-for-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

A template submission script will be provided soon.

To submit a job to the Dawn PVC partition, your batch script should look similar
to the following example:

```
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --job-name=my-batch-job
#SBATCH --account=<your Dawn SLURM project>
#SBATCH --partition=pvc9 # Dawn PVC partition
#SBATCH -n 4   # Number of tasks (usually number of MPI ranks)
#SBATCH -c 24  # Number of cores per task
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 # Number of requested GPUs per node

module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
# Set up environment below for example by loading more modules

srun <your_application>
```

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p pvc9
```

### Jobs requiring multiple nodes[¶](#jobs-requiring-multiple-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

## Dawn Documentation[¶](#dawn-documentation "Permalink to this headline")

## **AIRR**

**Welcome to Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal**

The Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal is a web-based platform developed by the University of Cambridge to provide secure, streamlined access to DAWN, one of the UK’s national AI Research Resource (AIRR) systems. It operates under the broader UKRI-supported AI Research Resource Federation (AIRRFED) initiative. A parallel facility, Isambard-AI, is hosted at the University of Bristol, forming the other half of the national AIRR infrastructure.

The portal enables researchers to:

* Request access to DAWN’s compute resources,
* Manage project membership and roles, and
* View and report on usage statistics.

This documentation provides a comprehensive guide to navigating the system, understanding its features, and utilizing its full capabilities.

**Overview of AIRRFED**

AIRRFED aims to provide secure, federated access to national AI and HPC infrastructures. This project is a collaborative effort involving multiple institutions, with Dawn at the University of Cambridge and Isambard-AI at the University of Bristol serving as key sites.

Authentication is federated via MyAccessID, allowing users to log in using their institutional credentials. Authorization and resource allocations are managed centrally through Waldur, which is integrated with the Atlassian Service Desk (JIRA) to automate access provisioning and operational workflows.

Principal Investigators (PIs) affiliated with UK universities can submit compute access requests to UKRI. Upon approval, projects are allocated to one of the two national AIRR sites, Cambridge (Dawn) or Bristol (Isambard-AI), based on suitability and availability.

**Getting Access**

Access to DAWN are allocated by bodies external. Prospective users will need to apply via these routes:

## Hardware[¶](#hardware "Permalink to this headline")

The Dawn (PVC) nodes are:

* 256 Dell PowerEdge XE9640 servers

each consisting of:

* 2x Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8468 (formerly codenamed Sapphire Rapids) (96 cores in total)
* 1024 GiB RAM
* 4x Intel(R) Data Center GPU Max 1550 GPUs (formerly codenamed Ponte Vecchio) (128 GiB GPU RAM each)
* Xe-Link 4-way GPU interconnect within the node
* Quad-rail NVIDIA (Mellanox) HDR200 InfiniBand interconnect

and each PVC GPU contains two stacks (previously known as tiles) and 1024 compute units.

Hello HPC support team,

Name: Liam Collins-Jones, Account: lc984, PI: Gemma Bale
I would like to install Toast++ software (http://web4.cs.ucl.ac.uk/research/vis/toast/download_src.html ). I attempted doing this myself towards the start of 2025 but was unsuccessful. Would anyone on your end be able to install this on my account? Happy to provide more details etc.
Thanks! Liam.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Source Build / User-Space Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help installing Toast++ in their own workspace after an earlier unsuccessful attempt. This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a request for a central software install. The best initial helpdesk response is to gather any previous build errors and, if needed, attempt a clean user-space build internally before sending tested instructions.

ACTION
1) Confirm the source/version the user is trying to build.
2) Ask for any error output from the user's earlier build attempt if available.
3) If needed, attempt a clean user-space build in `hpc-work` and return tested instructions.
4) Prefer a user-space install unless there is evidence of wider demand for a central package.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Liam,

This looks like a user-space source-build request rather than a central software installation.

If you still have the output from your previous build attempt, please send it to us. If needed, we can also try a clean build internally and then send you tested instructions for building Toast++ in your workspace.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Category: Software Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes

The user is requesting the installation of Toast++ software on the HPC system [1]. The software installation process was previously attempted by the user but was unsuccessful [1].

To install Toast++ software, the user can try installing it in their user space using a Python virtual environment [3]. The user can use conda or pip to install the software [3]. If the user encounters any issues during the installation process, they can contact the HPC support team for assistance [6].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please provide more details about the error messages you encountered during the previous installation attempts?
- Have you tried installing the software using a Python virtual environment?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To install Toast++ software, you can try installing it in your user space using a Python virtual environment. You can use conda or pip to install the software. If you encounter any issues during the installation process, please contact us for assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Software installations should be done in the user's space to avoid any potential conflicts with the system [3].

'# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

Login with your CSD3 HPC credentials: CRSid and password.

![Login page](../_images/ondemand-login.png)

Once completed you will be presented with this page:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page.png)

Or this page if your browser window is more compact:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page-compact.png)

The three-lined icon to the right of the compact page expands when clicked to show the full toolbar available on the non-compact page. At this point, it is possible to access the tools available in Login-Web.

## File Transfer and Management[¶](#file-transfer-and-management "Permalink to this headline")

This function allows the transfer of data to and from [Research Storage Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store) project areas, or your CSD3 home directory. To use the file transfer client, select `Files` in the main menu.

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-menu.png)

Login-Web provides a web-based File Explorer that can be used to upload and download files to/from your CSD3 home directory or project directory, and copy, delete, rename, and edit files. Below you can see a screenshot of the main interface:

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files.png)

### Navigating[¶](#navigating "Permalink to this headline")

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-goto.png)

By default, the file browser will be looking at your home directory. If you have access to a RDS or RCS project space, browse to the rds or rcs subdirectories of your home directory as normal; alternatively if you know the full path to the directory you can move stepwise through the path near the top of the window. The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:** AI chatbots do not have the same level of insight or familiarity with CSD3/Dawn as the human support team who maintain these systems.
* **Verify before you act:** Do not copy and run commands or instructions provided by AI models without review. Be especially cautious with:
  + Suggestions to use `sudo` commands or any other privilege escalation routes
  + Software installation steps that may be inappropriate or incompatible with our hardware/software stack(s)
  + Requests for login or sensitive credentials
* **Be security conscious:**
  + **Be aware of data retention:** Most AI assistants/websites retain or process data externally. Anything you share could be stored or used to improve the model; treat prompts as public information.
  + **Avoid sharing system-specific details:** Do not include configuration files, system paths, network layouts, or architecture diagrams that could expose internal infrastructure details.
  + **Do not upload logs or files directly:** These may contain sensitive metadata, user data, or internal identifiers that should remain confidential.

Tip

If you need to illustrate a problem, replace identifying information (e.g., usernames, project codes, or directory paths) with placeholders such as `<user>`, `<project>`, or `<path>`.

* **Protect your information:** Never include sensitive or identifying data in your prompts. This includes:
  + Account names
  + Passwords
  + SSH/API keys or other sensitive passcodes
  + IP addresses or hostnames
  + Any data protected under institutional, legal or intellectual property confidentiality'}, {'rank': 7, 'doc_id': 'HPCSSUP-63736::chunk::0000', 'score': 0.015625, 'source': 'tickets', 'retrieval_sources': ['tickets'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 4, 'dense_score': 0.7679019, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'ticket_memory', 'authority_tier': 1, 'validity_status': 'unknown', 'freshness_hint': '2024-04-02T17:07:11.563+0100', 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': [], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': [], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.015625, 'source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '[TICKET_SUMMARY] Installation of Relion 5

1.0}}, 'text': '# Sapphire Rapid Nodes[¶](#sapphire-rapid-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

*These new nodes entered general service in July 2023.*

## Slurm partitions[¶](#slurm-partitions "Permalink to this headline")

* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are named according to the scheme *cpu-r-[1-112]*.
* Some of the Sapphire Rapid nodes are in the **sapphire** Slurm partition. Your existing
  -CPU projects will be able to submit jobs to these.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes have **112 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and **4580 MiB RAM per cpu** for a total of 512 GB RAM per node.
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are interconnected by Mellanox NDR200 Infiniband
* The Sapphire Rapid nodes are running [Rocky Linux 8](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8).

## Recommendations for running on Sapphire Rapids[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r nodes are running Rocky 8, you may need to recompile
your applications.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-q-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 4:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p sapphire
```

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an
[interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids) and work on a sapphire partition node directly.

Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.

Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

The user is requesting to add a module for the latest version of blast, as the current version 2.11 is incomplete and missing some features like blastp [1]. According to the documentation [2], the modules provided by Spack behave differently from standard Linux modules, and each module has the versions of dependency libraries hardcoded using RPATH [5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To address the user's request, we need to check if a newer version of the blast module is available on the system. We can use the `module avail` command to list all available modules [2]. If a newer version is available, we can load it using the `module load` command [2]. If not, we may need to consider building a new module for the latest version of blast.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message or issue the user is experiencing with the current version of blast?
- Are there any specific dependencies or libraries required for the latest version of blast?

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When using modules provided by Spack, care must be taken to ensure that the correct versions of dependencies are loaded [5].
- It is essential to follow the documentation and guidelines for managing modules on the system [1][2].

'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

This command can be added in the .bashrc file for accessing the private modules without entering the above command everytime the user logins.

3. To list the private modules is done using the `modules av` command. The private modules list will be shown below the other global module lists.

To create group modules a folder with the module files and has to be created in the rds folder that is available to all the members of the group. Moreover, the program files that the modules need must the rds storage too. Both of these folders and files containted must have read permission by all users.

Each user in the group must create a symbolic link to the folder with the group module files. An example of this command is shown below. Please change the path to the group modules to the absolute path to the folder with your group’s modules.

```
cd ~/privatemodules
ln -s /rds/my-groups-project/my-id/groupmodules/ groupmodules
```

## Available Compiler Suites[¶](#available-compiler-suites "Permalink to this headline")

**Note:** As CSD3 uses dynamic linking by default you will generally also need
to load any modules you used to compile your code in your job submission
script when you run your code.

### Intel Compiler Suite[¶](#intel-compiler-suite "Permalink to this headline")

The Intel compiler suite is accessed by loading the `intel/bundles/complib/*` module. For example:

```
module load intel/bundles/complib/2017.4
```

Once you have loaded the module, the compilers are available as:

* `ifort` - Fortran
* `icc` - C
* `icpc` - C++

'text': '# Using Spack[¶](#using-spack "Permalink to this headline")

## Modules provided by Spack[¶](#modules-provided-by-spack "Permalink to this headline")

Care must be taken when using modules provided by Spack as they behave differently
from standard Linux modules.

The [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io) package management tool is used
to manage much of the software and libraries installed on CSD3. Spack allows
us to automatically resolve dependencies and have multiple versions of tested
software installed simultaneously without them interfering with each other.

To achieve this, Spack makes use of RPATH to hardcode the paths of dependencies
into libraries. This means that when you load a module for a particular library
you do not need to load any further modules for dependencies of that library.

For example, the *boost* toolkit depends on the MPI, zlib and bzip2 libraries:

```
boost@1.64.0
    ^bzip2@1.0.6
    ^mpich@2.14
    ^zlib@1.2.10
```

Spack arranges things so that if you load the boost module:

```
module load boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46
```

then you do not also need to load the bzip2, mpich and zlib modules.

This, however, can lead to behaviour that is unexpected for modules. For example,
on CSD3 there are two versions of zlib available: 1.2.8 and 1.2.10. You may
imagine that you can use boost with zlib 1.2.8 with the following commands:

```
module load zlib-1.2.8-gcc-6.2.0-epathtp
module load boost-1.64.0-gcc-6.2.0-pftxg46
```

'source_ranks': {'docs': 4}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# AlphaFold[¶](#alphafold "Permalink to this headline")

From the Alphafold website <https://github.com/deepmind/alphafold>

> This package provides an implementation of the inference pipeline of AlphaFold v2.0.
> This is a completely new model that was entered in CASP14 and published in Nature.
> For simplicity, we refer to this model as AlphaFold throughout the rest of this document.
>
> We also provide an implementation of AlphaFold-Multimer. This represents a work in progress and AlphaFold-Multimer isn’t expected to be as stable as our monomer AlphaFold system. Read the guide for how to upgrade and update code.

## AlphaFold data on CSD3[¶](#alphafold-data-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The 2.8TB dataset is stored in:

```
/datasets/public/AlphaFold/data
```

Note that you may need to ls the directory in order for it to be mounted. There are example sequences stored in the input directory.

The dataset has been recently updated (November 2023) so scripts may not work without pointing to new versions of the files. In addition, to address this [issue](https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold/issues/810), newer versions of the `uniref` can be found here /datasets/public/AlphaFold/data/uniref30/UniRef30\_2023\_02\*.

## Running AlphaFold2 on CSD3[¶](#running-alphafold2-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

There are various ways to run AlphaFold2 on CSD3. We encourage the use of:

To get up and running quickly on CSD3 it is possible to run the Singularity container provided as a module:

```
module load alphafold/2.3.2-singularity
```

See [Singularity](#singularity) for more information. This is not performant as it runs the slow CPU step and GPU step in sequence meaning that 4 GPUs are sitting idle for most of the time. Instead, see the [ParaFold section](#parafold-section) for instructions on how to obtain better performance.

If you would like us to support other implementations of AlphaFold2 or if anything here is unclear or incorrect please contact support.

## Separating CPU and GPU steps using ParallelFold and Conda[¶](#separating-cpu-and-gpu-steps-using-parallelfold-and-conda "Permalink to this headline")

ParaFold <https://parafold.sjtu.edu.cn> is a fork of AlphaFold2 that separates the CPU MSA step from the GPU prediction so that it can be executed in a two step process. This is more desirable because the GPU remains idle for most of the running time when using DeepMind’s Singularity build as shown [below](#singularity).

To install, create a Conda environment using CSD3’s Conda module or download it yourself:

```
module load miniconda/3
conda create -n parafold python=3.8
```

If downloading yourself, use [Miniforge](https://github.com/conda-forge/miniforge) as it is shipped with [mamba](https://github.com/mamba-org/mamba) which is an optimised implementation on Conda.

Then follow the instructions <https://github.com/RSE-Cambridge/ParallelFold>, with usage information [here](https://github.com/RSE-Cambridge/ParallelFold/blob/main/docs/usage.md). Note that this [fork](https://github.com/RSE-Cambridge/ParallelFold) is an optimised form of the [original](https://github.com/Zuricho/ParallelFold) to run on CSD3.

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Conda Environment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with activating a Conda environment in a SLURM script [1]. The environment is activated correctly on the login node, but the job still uses the base Conda Python when submitted to SLURM [1]. This issue is similar to previous cases where the Conda environment was not properly activated in the SLURM script [2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should modify the SLURM script to correctly activate the Conda environment. The recommended steps are:
1. Load the necessary modules, including Miniconda [2][3].
2. Source the `.bashrc` file to enable the Conda environment [5].
3. Activate the Conda environment using `conda activate` [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].
4. Verify that the correct Python version is being used [1].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact SLURM script and the error message you are receiving?
- Have you tried sourcing the `.bashrc` file before activating the Conda environment?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Stefanos,
To activate your Conda environment in the SLURM script, please try the following steps:
1. Load the necessary modules, including Miniconda.
2. Source the `.bashrc` file to enable the Conda environment.
3. Activate the Conda environment using `conda activate`.
4. Verify that the correct Python version is being used.
If you are still experiencing issues, please provide the exact SLURM script and the error message you are receiving.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When using Conda environments in SLURM scripts, it is essential to ensure that the environment is properly activated to avoid conflicts with the base Python installation [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].
- Users should be aware of the potential issues with loading multiple modules and activating Conda environments in SLURM scripts [2][3].

If this is the first time using Anaconda Python then it is important to
run the first time `conda init` command to correctly prepare your shell
environemnt for using the full suite of `conda` commands. This only needs
to be done once.

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ which conda
/usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/conda
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda init
[... list of modifications not made ...]
modified      /home/user/.bashrc

==> For changes to take effect, close and re-open your current shell. <==
```

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate` commands
to work.

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

# run script (change helloworld.py to your own)
python helloworld.py
```

## Running Tensorflow in containers[¶](#running-tensorflow-in-containers "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 also supports running inside apptainer containers. A Tensorflow container can be downloaded from the Docker Hub and run with:

```
# The next command generates a > 3.8 GB file in the folder it is issued. Use with care.
apptainer pull docker://tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu
apptainer exec --nv ./tensorflow_latest-gpu.sif python ./helloworld.py
